coredata.dc:title: Sensitive determination of hydroquinone and catechol using an electrochemical sensor based on nitrogen-doped malic acid carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: This study introduces an advanced electrochemical sensor fabricated by immobilizing nitrogen-doped malic acid carbon quantum dots (N-MCQDs) onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) via microwave-assisted synthesis and electrodeposition. The N-MCQDs were comprehensively characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, and atomic force microscopy (AFM), confirming their successful synthesis and uniform distribution on the GCE surface. The N-MCQDs-modified GCE electrode (N-MCQDs/GCE) sensor displayed a remarkable linear detection range of 1–500 μM for hydroquinone (HQ) and 1–200 μM for catechol (CC), with ultra-low detection limits of 0.18 μM for HQ and 0.13 μM for CC. It also exhibits commendable stability, interference resistance, and the capability to accurately measure in complex real sample. These superior characteristics were attributed to the enhanced electrical conductivity and increased active sites due to nitrogen doping. This study not only broadens the application spectrum of carbon quantum dots but also offers a novel perspective for the design of high-performance electrochemical sensors for environmental analysis.

head.ce:title: Sensitive determination of hydroquinone and catechol using an electrochemical sensor based on nitrogen-doped malic acid carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces an advanced electrochemical sensor fabricated by immobilizing nitrogen-doped malic acid carbon quantum dots (N-MCQDs) onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) via microwave-assisted synthesis and electrodeposition. The N-MCQDs were comprehensively characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, and atomic force microscopy (AFM), confirming their successful synthesis and uniform distribution on the GCE surface. The N-MCQDs-modified GCE electrode (N-MCQDs/GCE) sensor displayed a remarkable linear detection range of 1–500 μM for hydroquinone (HQ) and 1–200 μM for catechol (CC), with ultra-low detection limits of 0.18 μM for HQ and 0.13 μM for CC. It also exhibits commendable stability, interference resistance, and the capability to accurately measure in complex real sample. These superior characteristics were attributed to the enhanced electrical conductivity and increased active sites due to nitrogen doping. This study not only broadens the application spectrum of carbon quantum dots but also offers a novel perspective for the design of high-performance electrochemical sensors for environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the realm of advanced nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), confined to dimensions less than 10 nm, embody a distinctive subclass of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures. These materials retain the quintessential traits of carbon-based materials, such as high conductivity and stability. They also acquire unique properties from heteroatom doping and the presence of an array of oxygen-bearing surface functionalities [1–3]. Their amalgamation of exceptional characteristics, including pronounced fluorescence, superior optics, excellent aqueous dispersibility, negligible toxicity, eco-friendliness, facile synthesis from diverse precursors, and inherent biocompatibility, renders CQDs instrumental in pioneering applications across various forefronts of science and technology [4–6]. Specifically, in the progression of electrochemical sensor technologies, the multifaceted nature of CQDs has been harnessed to augment sensor design, enhancing both sensitivity and selectivity, and refining electron transport processes through their intrinsic conductivity and abundance of active surface sites. This has significantly augmented the detection proficiency for trace levels of a various of analytes [7].

Specific applications in environmental monitoring include the work of Li and colleagues [8], who engineered a nitrogen-doped CQD and graphene oxide composite-coated glassy carbon electrode (NCQDs-GO/GCE) proficient in simultaneous detection of Cd(II) and Pb(II), and that of Pizarro's group, who reported a green sensing platform using nafion-modified Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the sensitive quantification of cadmium and lead in seafood samples, attaining low detection thresholds of 100.893 nM and 40.975 nM, respectively [9]. In addition, Liu et al. [10], combined CQDs derived from poplar leaves with the metal-organic framework ZIF-8, achieved an unprecedented detection limit of 0.04 nM for Pb2+, showcasing the remarkable potential of CQDs in trace analysis. R. Atchudan and A. K. Sundramoorthy's research groups have synthesized a series of CQDs using natural biomass sources, including Coccinia grandis fruit, Ficus religiosa bark, Damask rose flower petals, and Singapore Cherry [11–14]. These biomass-derived CQDs exhibit high sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+ ions. CQDs are also making significant strides in pharmaceutical analysis. Periyat's team fabricated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots through a one-pot hydrothermal process with polyindole, enabling ultralow detection of dopamine at 0.15 nM on a GCE [15]. Meanwhile, Nguyen's research employed CQD-functionalized electrodes for the highly sensitive detection etoposide, contributing to advancements in monitoring residual levels of this antitumor drug [16]. The utility of CQDs extends prominently to the field of biomarker sensing [17,18]. Notably, Shabani Shayeh's team devised an ultrasensitive electrochemical aptasensor based on Au/CQD composites for prostate-specific antigen, achieving a detection limit of merely 2 fg mL−1, marking a pivotal step in prostate cancer diagnostics [19]. Furthermore, studies like Moço's on HIV RNA sensors [20], Wang's work on sulfamethoxazole detection [21], Ye's research on Salmonella antibiotic sensors [22], and Lakshmanakumar's innovation in cardiac troponin I sensing without antibodies collectively exemplify the pivotal role of CQDs in developing high-performance, biomarker-specific detection systems [23].

With accelerated industrialization and modernization, pollution of aqueous environments by phenolic compounds, particularly hydroquinone (HQ) and catechol (CC), has emerged as a pressing global environmental challenge. These compounds pose severe threats to ecological balance and may accumulate through the food chain, posing potential risks to human health [24,25]. Although a gamut of techniques has been explored, including chromatographic, fluorometric, molecularly imprinted, and electrochemical methods et al. [26–31], the application of CQDs in electrochemical detection for HQ and CC still presents untapped potential. Tang and coworkers demonstrated simultaneous electrochemical detection of HQ and CC after electropolymerizing CQDs on a GCE, with detection ranges spanning 0.5–100 μM for both analytes [32]. Complementary efforts by Hu's team, who capitalized on carbon dot/reduced graphene oxide hybrids to modify electrodes, revealed a substantial sensitivity boost through optimized electronic interactions, broadening the linear detection ranges to 0.5–1000 μM for HQ and 1.0–950 μM for CC [33], thereby affirming the nascent yet promising role of CQDs in such detections.

Due to the greater electronegativity of nitrogen compared to carbon, doping nitrogen into CQDs can significantly alter their electronic structure, leading to an upward shift in the Fermi level. This change induces new energy levels within the band structure of the CQDs, which serve as bridges for electron transitions, effectively reducing the activation energy required for carriers to overcome energy barriers, thereby significantly enhancing the electrical conductivity of the carbon quantum dots [34]. Moreover, the incorporation of nitrogen atoms creates specific defects in the CQDs, which not only increase the concentration of free carriers but also provide additional active sites for the adsorption of target molecules, thus enhancing the sensor's selectivity and sensitivity in identifying and binding with target analytes [35]. Motivated by these benefits, this study embarked on a microwave-assisted synthesis strategy to produce nitrogen-doped malic acid-based CQDs (N-MCQDs). The selection of malic acid as a precursor is pivotal for two reasons: first, it facilitates the efficient formation of CQDs; second, it inherently introduces oxygen-containing functional groups, which are essential for the solubility and stability of the N-MCQDs in aqueous environments. These functional groups play a critical role in the subsequent immobilization of N-MCQDs on a glassy carbon electrode via electrodeposition, ultimately enabling the creation of an advanced N-MCQDs-modified electrode (N-MCQDs/GCE) for sophisticated sensing applications. This research findings confirm that the N-MCQDs/GCE demonstrates remarkable enhancements in electron transfer kinetics and reductions in electrochemical impedance compared to the unmodified GCE. Additionally, it was found to possess broad linear detection ranges and low detection limits for both HQ and CC.

HQ, CC, and l-malic acid were procured from Aladin Industrial Corporation Ltd. (China). Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18 MΩ cm at 25 °C was used throughout the experiments.

l-malic acid (5 g) and urea (5 g)were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water under magnetic stirring to ensure complete dissolution. The mixture was then subjected to 2.45 GHz microwave irradiation for 20 min to induce rapid carbonization. Following cooling, unreacted materials were removed by centrifugation, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane for further purification. The filtrate was dialyzed against deionized water using a 1000 Da dialysis membrane for 24 h to thoroughly eliminate small molecular impurities. The solution post-dialysis was freeze-dried, yielding brown powdered N-MCQDs.

The GCE with an effective area of 7 mm2 was meticulously polished with a sequence of alumina slurries ranging from 1.0 to 0.05 μm, followed by ultrasonic cleaning in ultrapure water and nitrogen drying to ensure cleanliness. Thereafter, the pretreated GCE, designated as the working electrode, was immersed in a pH 5.5 NaH₂PO₄ - Na₂HPO₄ (PP) buffer solution saturated with 1 g∙mL−1 of N-MCQDs, along with a Hg/Hg2Cl2 reference electrode and a platinum wire auxiliary electrode. A series of cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans with a potential range of -1 – 2 V and a scan rate of 100 mV∙s−1 were executed to promote N-MCQDs immobilization onto the GCE substrate. Unbound species were rigorously cleansed, culminating in the fabrication of the N-MCQDs/GCE composite electrode.

To comprehensively elucidate the physicochemical properties of N-MCQDs, a suite of advanced analytical instruments was employed. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a AVATAR 360 (Nicolet, USA) using potassium bromide pellets in the scanning range of 4000 to 400 cm⁻1 with a resolution of 4 cm⁻1 to reveal functional group information. Raman spectroscopic investigations were executed on a LabRAM HR Evolution instrument (HORIBA Scientific, Japan), utilizing a 785 nm laser source, covering a wavenumber range from 50 to 4000 cm⁻1. Crystallographic features of N-MCQDs were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) utilizing a MiniFlex 600 system (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5418 Å), scanning over a 2θ range of 5–50° at a step size of 0.02° under operating conditions of 40 kV and 30 mA. The intricate surface morphology of N-MCQDs was scrutinized through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a JEM-F200 microscope (JEOL, Japan), operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, offering insights into the quantum dots' microstructural details. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze the elemental composition and chemical states of the samples. This was accomplished on a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), employing an Al Kα radiation source (1486.6 eV) under ultra-high vacuum conditions (>5.0 × 10⁻⁷ mBar). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed on a Dimension Icon (Bruker, Germany), providing high-resolution images of both bare GCE and N-MCQDs/GCE surfaces, with a scan size of 10 × 10 μm. The Zeta potential and Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of N-MCQDs were performed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (UK).

In the electrochemical detection studies of HQ and CC, a 0.2 mol∙L−1 PP buffer solution served as the electrolyte to maintain system stability and consistency in electrochemical reactions. CV experiments were conducted within a potential window from −0.2 to 0.8 V, while differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) employed refined parameters: a potential range of −0.1 to 0.5 V, amplitude of 0.05 V, pulse width of 0.05 s, and pulse period of 0.5 s to optimize signal resolution and minimize background interference. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements spanned frequencies from 0.01 Hz to 10 kHz with a 5 mV perturbation voltage, aiming to precisely evaluate charge transfer kinetics at the electrode interface. All tests were executed at room temperature to maintain uniform experimental conditions. The relationship between the anodic peak current (I pa) and the analyte concentration (c0) was quantified using the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (1)) [36]:
I pa = 2.69 × 105n3/2AD1/2co v 1/2
where I pa represents the anodic peak current, n denotes the number of electrons transferred, A signifies the electroactive surface area of the electrode (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2∙s−1), c0 is the solution concentration (mol∙L−1), and ν is the scan rate (V∙s−1). The main flow of the experimental procedure is shown in Scheme 1 .

HRTEM analysis divulges (Fig. 1 A) that N-MCQDs exhibit a spherical morphology with uniform particle sizes averaging approximately 2 nm. A meticulous inspection of individual N-MCQDs reveals distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.187 nm, corresponding to the (102) plane of graphite crystal, attesting to the periodicity and crystalline nature of the internal carbon atom arrangement in N-MCQDs. Furthermore, XRD analysis of an ensemble of N-MCQDs (Fig. S1) discloses a broad peak at 20.3°, assignable to the (002) plane of graphite, indicative of the characteristic layered stacking mode along the c-axis [37]. The breadth of this peak intimates a reduced orderliness of the layered structure or variability in interlayer distances, phenomena plausibly attributed to nitrogen doping effects and the quantum confinement inherent to these nanoparticles [38]. Jointly, HRTEM and XRD results affirm not only the uniform dimensions and highly ordered lattice alignment of N-MCQDs at the nanoscale but also disclose planar features akin to typical graphitic materials in their internal structure. Nevertheless, these traits are nuanced by quantum confinement effects and the singular influence of nitrogen doping, manifesting in structural properties that diverge from those of undoped or larger graphite materials, exhibiting greater complexity.

Spectral interrogation via infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1B) reveals a cornucopia of surface-bound functional moieties. Peaks centered at 3440 and 3189 cm−1 bear witness to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, inferring the presence of hydroxyl and amine functionalities [39–41]. A conspicuous peak at 1716 cm−1 signifies CO stretching, accompanied by bands at 1402 and 1350 cm−1 indicative of C–N stretches and accompanying C–H deformations. Additional features at 1286 and 1215 cm−1 denote bending vibrations of C–O and C–N bonds, while the peak at 1174 cm−1 is consonant with C–O–C stretching modes.

XPS spectra offer a profound insight into the rich chemical composition of the N-MCQDs surface. Within the XPS survey spectrum, characteristic peaks at 284.8, 399.8, and 531.8 eV correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. S2). A detailed examination of the C 1s peak distinguishes four distinct chemical environments of carbon (Fig. 1C): O–CO at 288.2 eV, C–N/C–O at 286.2 eV, C–C/C–H at 285.2 eV, and CC at 284.4 eV [42]. The N 1s fitting peaks are observed at 399.38 and 398.68 eV (Fig. S3), corresponding to the N–C bond and N–H bond, respectively [43]. Regarding oxygen (Fig. S4), the peak at 531.6 eV is assigned to CO, while the peak at 532.8 eV is associated with C–OH functionalities [44]. These findings collectively provide a comprehensive understanding of the surface chemistry of N-MCQDs, revealing the presence of various functional groups and the successful nitrogen doping, which are crucial for their enhanced electrochemical performance.

The Raman spectroscopic features of N-MCQDs (Fig. 1D) unveil pivotal insights into their structural integrity and functional attributes. The prominent G peak at 1570 cm⁻1 signifies the ordered arrangement of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms akin to graphite, underpinning the superior electrical conductivity of N-MCQDs. Complementarily, the D peak at 1368 cm⁻1 highlights internal disorder and defects, typically originating from the edges or discontinuities in the sp2-carbon framework. Notably, the intensity ratio of the G peak to the D peak (I G/I D) is 1.1, a value greater than 1 emphasizes the dominance of order in the sp2 carbon atoms within N-MCQDs. Even after nitrogen doping, the material retains the integrity of its graphene-like structure, which is crucial for maintaining its electrical conductivity. More importantly, the moderate defects introduced during nitrogen doping actually increase the number of electrochemically active sites in N-MCQDs. This dual benefit, maintaining high conductivity whilst providing additional active sites, is paramount for boosting the performance of electrochemical sensors. The Zeta potential of N-MCQDs in aqueous solution is 7.30 mV at pH 5.5 and −14.40 mV at pH 7.0 (Fig. 1E). This shift from positive to negative Zeta potential suggests the presence of amine groups (protonated at lower pH) and carboxyl groups (dissociated to –COO⁻ at higher pH) on the N-MCQDs surface. Therefore, it can be further inferred that the surface of N-MCQDs primarily contains amine (–NH₂) and carboxyl (–COOH) functional groups. DLS measurements indicate that the average particle size of N-MCQDs is 2.857 nm (Fig. 1F), which is consistent with TEM observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymerization of N-MCQDs on the surface of GCE
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A, a polished bare GCE was placed in a pH 5.5, 0.2 mol L−1 PP buffer solution without N-MCQDs and subjected to 15 cycles of CV scanning from −1 to 2 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. No significant redox peaks or current increases were observed on the bare GCE. Furthermore, there was only a slight increase in the electrode surface current before and after the scans, indicating that the PP buffer solution does not alter the electrochemical properties of the bare GCE surface. When the same experiment was conducted with a 1 mg mL−1 N-MCQDs solution, the first cycle of CV showed a similar curve shape to the one without N-MCQDs, but a weak reduction peak appeared at −0.2 V. After 15 cycles, the reduction peak at −0.2 V gradually increased, and an oxidation peak appeared at 1.5 V. In the absence of N-MCQDs, no such redox peaks were observed on the GCE. This indicates that the N-MCQDs were polymerized onto the electrode surface. As the number of cycles increased, more N-MCQDs deposited on the GCE, providing more active sites, which led to a gradual increase in the peak currents at these two potential positions. To further confirm that the N-MCQDs were indeed electropolymerized onto the GCE surface, the GCE was re-scanned in the pure PP buffer solution without N-MCQDs after the N-MCQDs electropolymerization. As shown in Fig. 2B, the CV curve shape was consistent with the one obtained during the polymerization of N-MCQDs, indicating that the electrode surface was no longer the initial bare surface but was covered with N-MCQDs. Previous literature has confirmed that the –NH2 group undergoes a process during CV scanning, initially forming a radical •NH, which subsequently reacts with activated carbon on the electrode surface to form covalent bonds, leading to the polymerization of amino compounds on the electrode [45,46]. Given that the surface of N-MCQDs contains –NH2 groups, it is hypothesized that their polymerization on the GCE surface follows the similar mechanism.

Additionally, a comprehensive analysis based on AFM reveals striking disparities in surface topography between the bare GCE and its N-MCQDs/GCE counterpart. The AFM imagery of the unmodified GCE distinctly portrays its relatively smooth surface (Fig. 2C), with height fluctuations spanning from −9.6 to 7.5 nm and an average roughness (Ra) value of 1.69 nm, reflecting the inherent smooth characteristics of the substrate material. After undergoing polymerization, N-MCQDs are observed to uniformly coat the GCE surface, forming a stable film-like structure (Fig. 2D). At this point, the surface height distribution of the N-MCQDs/GCE broadens to encompass the range from −17.9 to 14.6 nm, indicative of a more intricate surface architecture. Correspondingly, the Ra of the N-MCQDs/GCE escalates notably to 3.50 nm, marking a substantial increase over the bare GCE. This indicates that the deposition of N-MCQDs significantly alters the microscopic appearance of the electrode surface, providing more active sites for subsequent electrochemical reactions.

Fig. 3 A presents a comparative electrochemical study of K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 on an unmodified GCE and an N-MCQDs/GCE. A conspicuous enhancement in both the oxidation and reduction peak currents is discernible for the N-MCQDs/GCE, testifying to a marked improvement in the electrode's electrochemical activity following the modification. This amplification directly attests to the efficacy of the N-MCQDs layer in facilitating electron transfer processes, thereby augmenting the kinetic profile. A subtle yet noteworthy variation is observed in the peak potential separation (ΔE) between the oxidation and reduction events, with the N-MCQDs/GCE exhibiting a more symmetrical peak profile. This symmetry signifies an elevated degree of reversibility in the electrochemical reactions, reflecting favorably on the modified electrode's performance. Further quantitative assessment based on the Randles-Sevcik equation reveals that the electroactive surface area of the N-MCQDs/GCE expands by 15.5 % relative to the GCE [36]. This finding reinforces the notion that the introduction of N-MCQDs not only bolsters the conductivity of the electrode but also effectively enlarges its surface area, thereby heightening electron transfer efficiency and amplifying the current response.

Inspection of the Nyquist plot in Fig. 3B reveals a significant diminution in the semicircle diameter at higher frequencies for the N-MCQDs/GCE compared to the unmodified GCE, while in the low-frequency region, the linear segment of N-MCQDs/GCE exhibits a steeper slope [47–51].To further investigate these EIS features, equivalent circuit modeling was applied to the EIS datasets for both electrodes, with the fitted results depicted in Fig. 3C (GCE's EIS) and D (N-MCQDs/GCE's EIS), and the respective fitting parameters summarized in Table 1 . From the tabulated data, it is evident that the charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the GCE stands at 156.5 Ω, which notably reduces to 72.2 Ω upon N-MCQDs modification. This decrement signifies a substantial alleviation of charge transfer resistance, implying that the N-MCQDs coating facilitates an accelerated rate of electrochemical reactions and unhindered charge transfer at the electrode surface. Concerning the constant phase element time constant (CPE-T), the N-MCQDs/GCE displays a value of 1.02 × 10−5 s, a marked increase from the GCE's 4.32 × 10−6 s, highlighting an enhanced capacitive effect at the modified electrode surface, conducive to enhanced charge storage and release. This observation aligns with the heightened current response exhibited by the N-MCQDs/GCE in CV curves. The constant phase element power (CPE-P), which gauges the deviation of the CPE from ideal capacitor behavior with values ranging between 0 and 1 (closer to 1 representing behavior closer to an ideal capacitor), decreases from 0.83 for the GCE to 0.78 for the N-MCQDs/GCE, suggesting a more pronounced non-ideal capacitive behavior post-modification. This may be attributed to an increased roughness of the electrode interface due to N-MCQDs deposition. Regarding the Warburg resistance (W–R), the N-MCQDs/GCE registers a slightly elevated value of 2843 Ω compared to the GCE's 2632 Ω, hinting at a modest increase in diffusion resistance. Paradoxically, the Warburg time constant (W-T) fitting results show a substantial decrease from 22.69 s for the GCE to 13.23 s for the N-MCQDs/GCE. This implies that, despite slight diffusion hindrances, the overall diffusion efficiency of the modified electrode is actually enhanced. This suggests that the N-MCQDs modification introduces a more intricate electrode surface topology, which, on one hand, complicates diffusion pathways leading to increased direct resistance, but on the other hand, potentially opens up additional diffusion channels or active sites, thereby accelerating diffusion and reducing the Warburg time constant. The increase in the Warburg parameter (W–P) also signals an increased frequency dependence of the diffusion process after modification, further attesting to the differentiated diffusion mechanisms between the pristine and modified GCE. Overall, the N-MCQDs modification exerts a profound positive impact on the electrochemical performance of the glassy carbon electrode, evidenced by reduced charge transfer resistance, bolstered capacitance, and optimized diffusion characteristics.

A meticulous comparison of Fig. 3E and F illuminates a pronounced augmentation in the electrochemical responsiveness of the N-MCQDs/GCE in contrast to its unadorned counterpart for the detection of HQ and CC. The enhanced peak currents for the redox processes of HQ and CC at the N-MCQDs/GCE directly substantiate the effectiveness of the N-MCQDs layer in expanding the effective surface area, enhancing electrical conductivity, and increasing the density of active sites, thereby significantly amplifying the electrochemical sensitivity to targeted analytes. Specifically, at the N-MCQDs/GCE, the anodic and cathodic peak potentials for HQ adjust to 0.242 V and 0.183 V, respectively, leading to a peak potential difference of 0.059 V. This is in stark contrast to the 0.157 V difference observed at the GCE, indicative of a more symmetrical redox process at the modified electrode, a reduced charge transfer resistance, and an optimized reaction kinetics. A parallel trend is discernible for CC, with the peak potential difference at the N-MCQDs/GCE being attenuated to 0.048 V (anodic peak at 0.339 V and cathodic peak at 0.291 V), markedly lower than the 0.179 V observed at the GCE. Notably, the anodic peak potentials of both HQ and CC at the N-MCQDs/GCE are shifted to more negative potentials relative to the GCE, suggesting a diminishment in overpotential and an acceleration of the redox reactions. In the context of detecting a binary mixture encompassing 100 μM HQ and CC (Fig. 3G), the supremacy of the N-MCQDs/GCE becomes even more pronounced. Unlike the GCE where the overlapping anodic peaks at 0.388 V engender convoluted responses for HQ and CC, the N-MCQDs/GCE facilitates a clear discrimination of the anodic peaks (HQ at 0.228 V and CC at 0.334 V) and cathodic peaks (HQ at 0.179 V and CC at 0.289 V), manifesting the capability of the N-MCQDs/GCE for simultaneous recognition and sensitive detection of HQ and CC.

Compared to CV, DPV is renowned for its heightened sensitivity, superior attenuation of background current, and enhanced resolution. Accordingly, in the DPV analysis of a 100 μM binary solution of HQ and CC (Fig. 3H), the N-MCQDs/GCE exhibits markedly superior performance relative to the unmodified GCE. At the N-MCQDs/GCE, the DPV peaks for HQ and CC are discernibly delineated at 0.166 and 0.270 V, respectively, with a peak potential disparity of 0.104 V, indicative of the modified electrode's efficacy in distinguishing between these two analytes. Contrariwise, the GCE yields a peak potential difference of merely 0.088 V, resulting in peak overlap and compromising the precision of individual identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of HQ and CC by N-MCQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the N-MCQDs layer deposited on the surface of the GCE has a significant impact on the electrode's electrochemical performance. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the variations in peak currents for HQ and CC are showcased across different polymerization cycle counts. It is noteworthy that as the number of polymerization cycles increases, the peak currents of both HQ and CC exhibit an initial rise followed by a decline, with maximum values attained specifically at the 11th polymerization cycle (Fig. 4B). Based on these observations, all further experimental procedures described in this study were conducted under the 11th polymerization cycle conditions.

Given the potential for significant influence exerted by the solution's pH on the ionization state of HQ and CC, as well as the electrochemical environment at the N-MCQDs/GCE surface, an in-depth investigation was conducted using the DPV method. It was discovered that within the pH range of 4.5–6.5, a strong linear negative correlation exists between the E p of HQ and CC with pH (Fig. 4C). Specifically, the linear equation for HQ was delineated as E p = −0.0612 pH + 0.5492 (R 2 = 0.9914), while that for CC was characterized by E p = −0.0612 pH + 0.6458 (R 2 = 0.9918). Both equations exhibited a consistent slope of −0.0612 V/pH, closely paralleling the theoretical value of −0.059 V/pH as predicted by the Nernst equation. According to previously reported literature [52,53], this near-perfect agreement signifies that HQ and CC undergo a two-electron, two-proton reaction process, and their reaction mechanisms are shown in Scheme 2 . Furthermore, upon an increase in pH, the peak currents for both HQ and CC initially ascended before experiencing a subsequent decline, with the apex reached at pH 5.5 (Fig. 4D). Consequently, a PP buffer solution at pH 5.5 was selected as the standard condition to regulate the system's acid-base equilibrium.

Further investigations revealed that the electrochemical behavior of HQ and CC at the N-MCQDs/GCE interface exhibits a strong linear correlation with ν 1/2 (Fig. 5 A). This relationship is quantified by the equations: for HQ, I pa = 0.6177 ν 1/2 - 0.7913 (R 2 = 0.9952) and I pc = −0.7998 ν 1/2 + 0.8322 (R 2 = 0.9953); for CC, I pa = 0.7572 ν 1/2 - 0.6457 (R 2 = 0.9971) and I pc = −0.5235 ν 1/2 + 0.7819 (R 2 = 0.9953). This linear interdependence is in strict conformity with the theoretical tenets of the Randles-Sevcik equation [36], affirming that within the investigated spectrum of scan rates, the kinetic velocity of the electrochemical reactions involving HQ and CC is fundamentally governed by the diffusion dynamics of the analytes toward the electrode surface. Notably, CC exhibits a steeper anodic peak current slope than HQ, suggesting it diffuses more readily to the electrode surface for oxidation under the same experimental conditions. Conversely, the gentler slope of the cathodic peak current of CC indicates a slower rate of CC returning to the solution after reduction at the electrode surface. These observations emphasize a more intimate interaction between CC and N-MCQDs, as opposed to HQ, at the N-MCQDs/GCE interface. This differential interaction strength is pivotal in shaping the distinct electrochemical behaviors of CC and HQ on the N-MCQDs/GCE.

In the analytical assessment employing DPV, the N-MCQDs/GCE displayed remarkable discriminative capabilities for the detection of HQ and CC in mixed standard solutions. With CC concentration held constant at 10.0 μM, the peak current of HQ showed a pronounced and concentration-dependent increase as HQ concentration rose. Conversely, the peak current of CC showed a negligible increment in response to varying HQ concentrations, underlining the minimal interference from HQ on CC's response (Fig. 5B). Further scrutiny revealed a linear correlation between HQ's peak current and its concentration within the range of 1–500 μM (Fig. 5C), characterized by the equation I p (μA) = 0.0888 C (μM) + 1.3103 (R 2 = 0.9978), with a detection limit of 0.18 μM (S/N = 3). Similarly, in the presence of 10.0 μmol/L HQ, CC's peak current increased proportionally with its own concentration, while changes in HQ concentration had a negligible effect on CC's peak current (Fig. 5D). The linear response range for CC spanned from 1 to 200 μM (Fig. 5E), with the corresponding equation I p (μA) = 0.1392 C (μM) + 0.6336 (R 2 = 0.9963) and a detection limit of 0.13 μM (S/N = 3).

Table 2 summarizes the linear range and lower detection limits of several modified glassy carbon electrodes reported in recent years for the detection of HQ and CC. From the table, it can be seen that although some electrodes, as shown in literature [55,60], and [61], can achieve exceptionally wide detection ranges or extremely sensitive detection limits. These superior performances are often accompanied by complex and expensive preparation processes for the electrode materials, which to some extent restrict their popularization and application. The electrode mentioned in literature [32] has a relatively simple preparation process, but its detection range is narrower. In comparison, the N-MCQDs/GCE composite electrode achieves a better balance among detection range, detection limit, and preparation cost, and is also more suitable for practical application scenarios.

Ensuring the reliability of analytical determinations hinges critically on electrode stability. To this end, a continuous three-week monitoring regimen was executed to assess the stability of the N-MCQDs/GCE. Throughout each week, a consistent experimental routine was followed: five consecutive DPV tests were performed on a 100.0 μM mixture of HQ and CC. Post-testing, the electrode underwent rigorous cleaning with ultrapure water and subsequent drying under nitrogen, prior to being preserved at 4 °C until the corresponding time point of the ensuing week for another set of five consecutive tests. The compiled outcomes are delineated in Table 3 . Over the course of three weeks, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the peak currents for HQ sequentially registered as 1.1 %, 1.5 %, and 1.7 %, whereas those for CC were recorded at 0.8 %, 1.3 %, and 1.5 %, respectively. Aggregating the data across the entire three-week duration, the cumulative RSDs for HQ and CC stood at 1.8 % and 2.2 %, respectively. Irrespective of whether evaluated within individual weekly replicates or cumulatively over the entire tri-weekly timeframe, the data uniformly attested to the superior stability of the N-MCQDs/GCE electrode.

Interference experiments, a cornerstone metric in assessing electrode performance, is essential for ensuring analytical accuracy. To evaluate this aspect, we designed an experiment involving a 100.0 μM HQ and CC mixture, to which we added ten common ions (Mg2⁺, Ca2⁺, K⁺, Na⁺, Fe³⁺, NO₃⁻, NH₄⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄2⁻) at 100-fold and six organics (glucose, p-nitrophenol, phenol, dopamine, ascorbic acid, uric acid) at 25-fold the HQ and CC concentrations, simulating complex environmental interference. Using the N-MCQDs/GCE electrode system, HQ and CC were tested individually. The test results showed that even in the presence of various potential interfering substances mentioned above, the N-MCQDs/GCE electrode maintained relative errors within ±5 % compared to the interference-free control (Fig. 5F). This finding unequivocally demonstrates that the N-MCQDs/GCE electrode possesses the capability to effectively mitigate interference, thereby maintaining a high degree of accuracy and reliability in the detection of HQ and CC, even in the face of challenges posed by diverse ions and organics.

A crucial benchmark for evaluating electrode practicability lies in its capacity for effective detection in actual samples. Accordingly, water from Guanyin Lake at Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University was selected as a model for real environmental samples. Initial analysis detected no traces of HQ or CC in the lake water. Subsequently, to assess the electrode's performance in a controlled yet realistic setting, a series of spiked recovery tests were conducted by deliberately adding known concentrations of HQ and CC into the water sample. The outcomes revealed that the N-MCQDs/GCE electrode achieved recovery rates for HQ and CC ranging from 92.7 % to 104.2 % and 91.3 %–101.3 %, respectively. These findings underscore the electrode's proficiency in conducting accurate and reliable quantitative analysis of HQ and CC in intricate water samples, effectively signifying its substantial potential for application in environmental monitoring contexts.

In summary, our research has innovatively engineered a nitrogen-doped malic acid-derived carbon quantum dots-modified glassy carbon electrode through a synergistic approach combining microwave-assisted synthesis with electrodeposition. The strategic incorporation of nitrogen dopants within the CQDs' structure has significantly elevated their electronic conductivity and surface activity, leading to superior electrochemical characteristics of the modified electrode. The N-MCQDs/GCE has been rigorously assessed and proven to be an efficacious and sensitive device for the concurrent detection of HQ and CC. It boasts extensive linear detection ranges—spanning 1–500 μM for HQ and 1–200 μM for CC—and achieves impressively low detection limits of 0.18 μM for HQ and 0.13 μM for CC. Moreover, the N-MCQDs/GCE exhibits commendable stability and robust interference resistance, along with proficient detection capabilities in real-world samples. These results confirm the significant advantages of nitrogen doping and the utilization of malic acid as a precursor in enhancing the performance of electrochemical sensors. They also provide a new perspective for broadening the application scope of carbon quantum dots and for the design of high-performance electrochemical sensors in the future.
Cheng Rong: Writing – original draft. Yanmei Huang: Data curation. Xinyu Zheng: Conceptualization.
